title: Divide whole numbers by fractions
description: Make sense of dividing whole numbers by non-unit fractions with a visual model.
topic_path: Module 2: Arithmetic operations including dividing by a fraction
Topic A: Dividing fractions by fractions

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the number line to answer the following $2$ questions.

[[☃ image 1]]

1. **How many groups of $\dfrac{5}{7}$ are in $1$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$8\div \dfrac{5}{7}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can count the number of groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ from the diagram. Since $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ is less than $1$, we will expect to find more than $1$ group.

The expression $\purpleD{8}\div \blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ can mean, "How many groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ are in $\purpleD{8}$?" There will be $\purpleD{8}$ times as many groups in $\purpleD{8}$ as there were in $1$.

###Counting the groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$

Since there are $7$ sevenths per $1$ unit, there are between $1$ and $2$ full groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$. 

It takes $5$ parts for each full group, and there are $2$ parts left over, for a total of $7$ out of $5$ parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are exactly $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$. 

We call $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ the **reciprocal** of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$.

###Finding the groups of $\dfrac{5}{7}$ in $\purpleD{8}$

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$. 

So there are $\purpleD{8}\times \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $\purpleD{8}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{8}\div \blueE{\dfrac{5}{7}} &= \purpleD{8} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac{8}{1}} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}\\\\
&= \goldE{\dfrac{56}{5}}
\end{align}$

###Summary

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{7}{5}}$ groups of ${\dfrac{5}{7}}$ in $1$. 

$8\div {\dfrac{5}{7}} = \goldE{\dfrac{56}{5}}$

options.alt: A number line from 0 to 11 sevenths with evenly spaced tick marks in increments of 1 seventh.  0 and 1 are labeled below the number line.  0 sevenths, 1 seventh, 2 sevenths, 3 sevenths, 4 sevenths, 5 sevenths, 6 sevenths, 7 sevenths, 8 sevenths, 9 sevenths, 10 sevenths, and 11 sevenths are labeled above the number line.  There is a teal line on the number line that begins at 0 sevenths and ends at 5 sevenths.  There is an orange line on the number line that begins at 5 sevenths and ends at 7 sevenths.  Above the number line is a fraction model with 10 equal parts.  5 parts are shaded teal, 2 parts are shaded orange, and 3 parts are white. The number of color blocks of the fraction model correspond to the length of the colored sections on the number line.

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the number line to answer the following $2$ questions.

[[☃ image 1]]

1. **How many groups of $\dfrac{3}{4}$ are in $1$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$5\div \dfrac{3}{4}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can count the number of groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ from the diagram. Since $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ is less than $1$, we will expect to find more than $1$ group.

The expression $\purpleD{5}\div \blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ can mean, "How many groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ are in $\purpleD{5}$?" There will be $\purpleD{5}$ times as many groups in $\purpleD{5}$ as there were in $1$.

###Counting the groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ in $1$

Since there are $4$ fourths per $1$ unit, there are between $1$ and $2$ full groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ in $1$. 

It takes $3$ parts for each full group, and there is $1$ part left over, for a total of $4$ out of $3$ parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are exactly $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ in $1$. 

We call $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$ the **reciprocal** of $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$.

###Finding the groups of $\dfrac{3}{4}$ in $\purpleD{5}$

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ in $1$. 

So there are $\purpleD{5}\times \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}}$ in $\purpleD{5}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{5}\div \blueE{\dfrac{3}{4}} &= \purpleD{5} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac{5}{1}} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}\\\\
&= \goldE{\dfrac{20}{3}}
\end{align}$

###Summary

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$ groups of ${\dfrac{3}{4}}$ in $1$. 

$5\div {\dfrac{3}{4}} = \goldE{\dfrac{20}{3}}$

options.alt: A number line from 0 to 2 with evenly spaced tick marks in increments of 1 fourth.  0, 1, and 2 are labeled below the number line.  0 fourths, 1 fourth, 2 fourths, 3 fourths, 4 fourths, 5 fourths, 6 fourths, 7 fourths, and 8 fourths are labeled above the number line.  There is a teal line on the number line that begins at 0 and ends at 3 fourths.  There is an orange line on the number line that begins at 3 fourths and ends at 1.  Above the number line is a fraction model with 6 equal parts.  3 parts are shaded teal, 1 part is shaded orange, and 2 parts are white. The number of color blocks of the fraction model correspond to the length of the colored sections on the number line.

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the number line to answer the following $2$ questions.

[[☃ image 1]]

1. **How many groups of $\dfrac{5}{3}$ are in $1$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$9\div \dfrac{5}{3}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can count the number of groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ from the diagram. Since $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ is more than $1$, we will expect to find less than $1$ group.

The expression $\purpleD{9}\div \blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ can mean, "How many groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ are in $\purpleD{9}$?" There will be $\purpleD{9}$ times as many groups in $\purpleD{9}$ as there were in $1$.

###Counting the groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ in $1$

Since there are $3$ thirds per $1$ unit, there are between $0$ and $1$ full groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ in $1$. 

It takes $5$ parts for each full group, and there are $3$ parts left over, for a total of $3$ out of $5$ parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are exactly $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ in $1$. 

We call $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$ the **reciprocal** of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$.

###Finding the groups of $\dfrac{5}{3}$ in $\purpleD{9}$

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ in $1$. 

So there are $\purpleD{9}\times \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}}$ in $\purpleD{9}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{9}\div \blueE{\dfrac{5}{3}} &= \purpleD{9} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac{9}{1}} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}\\\\
&= \goldE{\dfrac{27}{5}}
\end{align}$

###Summary

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{5}}$ groups of ${\dfrac{5}{3}}$ in $1$.

$9\div {\dfrac{5}{3}} = \goldE{\dfrac{27}{5}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the number line to answer the following $2$ questions.

[[☃ image 1]]

1. **How many groups of $\dfrac{9}{2}$ are in $1$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$4\div \dfrac{9}{2}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can count the number of groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ from the diagram. Since $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ is more than $1$, we will expect to find less than $1$ group.

The expression $\purpleD{4}\div \blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ can mean, "How many groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ are in $\purpleD{4}$?" There will be $\purpleD{4}$ times as many groups in $\purpleD{4}$ as there were in $1$.

###Counting the groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ in $1$

Since there are $2$ halves per $1$ unit, there are between $0$ and $1$ full groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ in $1$. 

It takes $9$ parts for each full group, and there are $2$ parts left over, for a total of $2$ out of $9$ parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are exactly $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ in $1$. 

We call $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}}$ the **reciprocal** of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$.

###Finding the groups of $\dfrac{9}{2}$ in $\purpleD{4}$

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ in $1$. 

So there are $\purpleD{4}\times \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}}$ in $\purpleD{4}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{4}\div \blueE{\dfrac{9}{2}} &= \purpleD{4} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac{4}{1}} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}}\\\\
&= \goldE{\dfrac{8}{9}}
\end{align}$

###Summary

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{9}}$ groups of ${\dfrac{9}{2}}$ in $1$.

$4\div {\dfrac{9}{2}} = \goldE{\dfrac{8}{9}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the number line to answer the following $2$ questions.

[[☃ image 1]]

1. **How many groups of $\dfrac{9}{5}$ are in $1$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$6\div \dfrac{9}{5}=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can count the number of groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ from the diagram. Since $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ is more than $1$, we will expect to find less than $1$ group.

The expression $\purpleD{6}\div \blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ can mean, "How many groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ are in $\purpleD{6}$?" There will be $\purpleD{6}$ times as many groups in $\purpleD{6}$ as there were in $1$.

###Counting the groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ in $1$

Since there are $5$ fifths per $1$ unit, there are between $0$ and $1$ full groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ in $1$. 

It takes $9$ parts for each full group, and there are $5$ parts left over, for a total of $5$ out of $9$ parts.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are exactly $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{9}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ in $1$. 

We call $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{9}}$ the **reciprocal** of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$.

###Finding the groups of $\dfrac{9}{5}$ in $\purpleD{6}$

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{9}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ in $1$. 

So there are $\purpleD{6}\times \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{9}}$ groups of $\blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}}$ in $\purpleD{6}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{6}\div \blueE{\dfrac{9}{5}} &= \purpleD{6} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{9}}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac{6}{1}} \times \maroonD{\dfrac{5}{9}}\\\\
&= \goldE{\dfrac{30}{9}}\\\\
&= \goldE{\dfrac{10}{3}}
\end{align}$

###Summary

There are $\maroonD{\dfrac{5}{9}}$ groups of ${\dfrac{9}{5}}$ in $1$.

$6\div {\dfrac{9}{5}} = \goldE{\dfrac{10}{3}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the number line to answer the following $2$ questions.

[[☃ image 1]]

1. **How many groups of $\dfrac{1}{4}$ are in $7$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$7\div \dfrac{3}{4}= $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can rewrite $\purpleD{7}$ as a number of fourths in order to find the number of groups of $\dfrac{1}{\tealE{4}}$ in $\purpleD{7}$.

The expression $\purpleD{7}\div {\dfrac{\maroonE{3}}{\tealE{4}}}$ can mean, "How many groups of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{3}}{\tealE{4}}}$ are in $\purpleD{7}$?" It takes $\maroonE{3}$ fourths to make a group of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{3}}{\tealE{4}}}$. So we can divide the number of fourths by $\maroonE{3}$.

###Counting the groups of ${\dfrac{1}{\tealE{4}}}$ in $\purpleD{7}$

We have $\purpleD{7}$ wholes. 

The number line shows $\tealE{4}$ fourths per whole. So we have $\purpleD{7} \times \tealE{4}$ fourths.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\redE{28}$ groups of $\dfrac{1}{\tealE{4}}$ in $\purpleD{7}$.

###Finding the groups of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{3}}{\tealE{4}}}$ in $\purpleD{7}$

It takes $\maroonE{3}$ fourths to make a group of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{3}}{\tealE{4}}}$. 

So we have $\purpleD{7} \times \tealE{4} \div \maroonE{3}$ groups of $\dfrac{\maroonE{3}}{\tealE{4}}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{7}\div \dfrac{\maroonE{3}}{4} &= \purpleD{7} \times \tealE{4} \div \maroonE{3}\\\\
&= \redE{28} \div \maroonE{3}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\redE{28}}{\maroonE{3}}
\end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

This makes sense, because the diagram shows $9$ full groups of $\dfrac{\maroonE{3}}{\tealE{4}}$ and $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of a group. That's equal to $\dfrac{\redE{28}}{\maroonE{3}}$ groups.

###Summary

There are $\redE{28}$ groups of ${\dfrac{1}{4}}$ in $7$. 

$7\div {\dfrac{3}{4}} = \dfrac{\redE{28}}{\maroonE{3}}$

options.alt: A number line from 0 to 7 and 1 half with evenly spaced tick marks in increments of 1 fourth.  0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7 are labeled below the number line.  0 fourths, 3 fourths, 6 fourths, 9 fourths, 12 fourths, 15 fourths, 18 fourths, 21 fourths, 24 fourths, 27 fourths, and 30 fourths are labeled above the number line.  Above the number line is a fraction model with 30 equal parts.  15 parts are shaded teal, 13 parts are shaded orange, and 2 parts are white. The number of color blocks of the fraction model correspond to the length of the colored sections on the number line.

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the number line to answer the following $2$ questions.

[[☃ image 1]]

1. **How many groups of $\dfrac{1}{8}$ are in $3$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$3\div \dfrac{5}{8}= $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can rewrite $\purpleD{3}$ as a number of eighths in order to find the number of groups of $\dfrac{1}{\tealE{8}}$ in $\purpleD{3}$.

The expression $\purpleD{3}\div {\dfrac{\maroonE{5}}{\tealE{8}}}$ can mean, "How many groups of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{5}}{\tealE{8}}}$ are in $\purpleD{3}$?" It takes $\maroonE{5}$ eighths to make a group of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{5}}{\tealE{8}}}$. So we can divide the number of eighths by $\maroonE{5}$.

###Counting the groups of ${\dfrac{1}{\tealE{8}}}$ in $\purpleD{3}$

We have $\purpleD{3}$ wholes. 

The number line shows $\tealE{8}$ eighths per whole. So we have $\purpleD{3} \times \tealE{8}$ eighths.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\redE{24}$ groups of $\dfrac{1}{\tealE{8}}$ in $\purpleD{3}$.

###Finding the groups of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{5}}{\tealE{8}}}$ in $\purpleD{3}$

It takes $\maroonE{5}$ eighths to make a group of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{5}}{\tealE{8}}}$. 

So we have $\purpleD{3} \times \tealE{8} \div \maroonE{5}$ groups of $\dfrac{\maroonE{5}}{\tealE{8}}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{3}\div \dfrac{\maroonE{5}}{\tealE{8}} &= \purpleD{3} \times \tealE{8} \div \maroonE{5}\\\\
&= \redE{24} \div \maroonE{5}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\redE{24}}{\maroonE{5}}
\end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

This makes sense, because the diagram shows $4$ full groups of $\dfrac{\maroonE{5}}{\tealE{8}}$ and $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of a group. That's equal to $\dfrac{\redE{24}}{\maroonE{5}}$ groups.

###Summary

There are $\redE{24}$ groups of ${\dfrac{1}{8}}$ in $3$. 

$3\div {\dfrac{5}{8}} = \dfrac{\redE{24}}{\maroonE{5}}$

assessment_items.question_content:

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] groups

2. **Evaluate.**

$2\div \dfrac{4}{9}= $ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Strategy

We can rewrite $\purpleD{2}$ as a number of ninths in order to find the number of groups of $\dfrac{1}{\tealE{9}}$ in $\purpleD{2}$.

The expression $\purpleD{2}\div {\dfrac{\maroonE{4}}{\tealE{9}}}$ can mean, "How many groups of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{4}}{\tealE{9}}}$ are in $\purpleD{2}$?" It takes $\maroonE{4}$ ninths to make a group of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{4}}{\tealE{9}}}$. So we can divide the number of ninths by $\maroonE{4}$.

###Counting the groups of ${\dfrac{1}{\tealE{9}}}$ in $\purpleD{2}$

We have $\purpleD{2}$ wholes. 

The number line shows $\tealE{9}$ ninths per whole. So we have $\purpleD{2} \times \tealE{9}$ ninths.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\redE{18}$ groups of $\dfrac{1}{\tealE{9}}$ in $\purpleD{2}$.

###Finding the groups of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{4}}{\tealE{9}}}$ in $\purpleD{2}$

It takes $\maroonE{4}$ ninths to make a group of ${\dfrac{\maroonE{4}}{\tealE{9}}}$. 

So we have $\purpleD{2} \times \tealE{9} \div \maroonE{4}$ groups of $\dfrac{\maroonE{4}}{\tealE{9}}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{2}\div \dfrac{\maroonE{4}}{\tealE{9}} &= \purpleD{2} \times \tealE{9} \div \maroonE{4}\\\\
&= \redE{18} \div \maroonE{4}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\redE{18}}{\maroonE{4}}
\end{align}$

[[☃ image 1]]

This makes sense, because the diagram shows $4$ full groups of $\dfrac{\maroonE{4}}{\tealE{9}}$ and $\dfrac{2}{4}$ of a group. That's equal to $\dfrac{\redE{18}}{\maroonE{4}}$ groups.

###Summary

There are $\redE{18}$ groups of ${\dfrac{1}{9}}$ in $2$. 

$2\div {\dfrac{4}{9}} = \dfrac{\redE{18}}{\maroonE{4}}$
